nativeRange.habitat: Commonly found in shallow lagoons and bays, typically over sand, rubble, or seagrass bottoms rather than directly on coral reefs; recorded at depths of about 1–20 m

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (at least 5 cm)

diet.notes: Has been documented foraging on sandy bottoms for small crustaceans, polychaete worms, and other benthic invertebrates. In captivity, generally takes vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, small krill, and quality marine pellet/flake foods readily. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly recommended for active foragers of this genus.

avoidWith: conspecifics (unless mated pair or harem with one male)
other Halichoeres wrasses in smaller tanks

summary: An active Indo-West Pacific sand-foraging wrasse reaching about 20 cm; generally considered an intermediate-level reef fish that needs a long footprint, a deep fine sand bed for burrowing, and a tightly covered tank to prevent jumping.
careNotes:
The Zig Zag Wrasse is an active, sand-associated species commonly found in shallow lagoons and seagrass beds across the Indo-West Pacific. In the aquarium it tends to spend much of its time cruising the open sand and rockwork in search of small invertebrate prey, so a long footprint and substantial open swimming area are generally advised over tall reef-style tanks.

A fine sand bed of at least 5 cm (2 in) depth is widely considered essential — like most Halichoeres, this species burrows into the sand to sleep and to escape perceived threats. Coarse gravel substrates have been reported to cause injury and are best avoided. A tightly fitting lid or mesh top is strongly recommended, as wrasses of this genus are well-documented jumpers.

Reef compatibility is generally considered acceptable with caution: the species has not been reported to bother stony or soft corals or tridacnid clams, but as it matures it has been known to consume ornamental shrimp, small hermit crabs, feather dusters, and tubeworms. It may also be useful as a natural predator of bristleworms, pyramidellid snails, and flatworms.

This species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite; in groups, a female will transition to male in the absence of a dominant male. It is best kept singly, as a mated pair, or as a small harem with one male and several females. Aggression toward conspecifics and similar-looking sand wrasses is commonly reported.

Cross-source disagreements were noted and averaged where possible. Adult size: LiveAquaria lists about 12.7 cm (5 in), while FishBase and ReefApp both report up to 20 cm TL — the FishBase figure is treated as authoritative for max size. Temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F), while FishBase reports a preferred wild range of 26.2–29.3°C; the recorded 24–27°C is an average across these sources. Tank size: LiveAquaria suggests 341 L (90 gal), ReefApp recommends 600 L, and aqua-fish.net recommends 500 L — given this is a 20 cm active sand forager that ranges widely, the recommendation is biased toward the larger end and rounded to 500 L.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely reported. In the wild, distinct pair formation occurs during breeding and the species is a pelagic spawner. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change from female to male can occur in groups lacking a dominant male.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for hobby care parameters: pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, 90 gal min tank, carnivorous, peaceful, reef safe, fine sand bed required, tight lid required. Adult size listed as 5 in (12.7 cm) — disagreed with FishBase/ReefApp 20 cm; averaged on the larger end.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria/misc, subfamily Julidinae), max size (20.0 cm TL), preferred temperature (26.2–29.3°C, mean 28.6), Indo-West Pacific range (Red Sea/East Africa to Papua New Guinea, Japan to GBR), habitat (shallow lagoons over sand/rubble/seagrass), behavior (usually solitary, aggressive to conspecifics), and reproduction (pair-forming pelagic spawner). IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (20 cm), minimum tank (600 L), substrate requirement (≥5 cm fine sand), protogynous sex change (female→male), known jumper, reef safe with caution (eats tubeworms and small inverts), transport-sensitive, may move rocks/corals. Used because the assigned secondary (Reef2Reef forum thread) returned HTTP 403.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Genus Profile: Halichoeres
additional.notes: Used for genus-level husbandry context: most Halichoeres need larger aquariums for active swimming, ≥2 in sand bed for burrowing, jump-resistant lid required, reef-safe with caution regarding ornamental crustaceans.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding not widely reported. Wild reproduction involves distinct pair formation and pelagic spawning. Protogynous (female-first) sex change occurs in groups.
